narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've already shown remarkable determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer despite the challenges many face in this field. Your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.70 stands out well above the typical student's average of 3.21, reflecting your academic excellence coming out of college. You're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants, and your family's middle-income background aligns with many others in the dataset. However, your Year 1 school decile of 1 and Year 3 decile of 2 are lower than the averages of around 5-6, suggesting your high school grades placed you toward the bottom of your class. Your LSAT score of 28 also falls notably below the typical 37 seen in the data. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 56.5% chance that you would fail it—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed. The most influential factor pulling against you was your Year 3 decile rank, which strongly signaled a higher risk of bar failure compared to peers with stronger high school records. Your Year 1 decile and LSAT score also contributed toward that concern, as they deviated from the higher levels associated with bar passers. On a brighter note, your outstanding undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive pull, pushing the prediction toward success more than any other factor, while your family income and full-time status offered modest support. Overall, while your college performance shines, the model weighed your earlier academic deciles and LSAT as bigger red flags when stacked against typical admitted students who boast stronger high school rankings and test scores. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, gathering stronger letters of recommendation that highlight your undergraduate strengths, or exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your unique background as a Black woman. You're clearly capable—many have overcome similar hurdles to pass the bar and thrive. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
